explicit_text: Yamato Colony was founded through great hardships by Mr. Kyutaro Abiko of Nichibei. The land was difficult—sandy soil with little water—and many settlers struggled, some even dying during the colony's development. Despite these challenges, the colony grew into a fine ranch, and we are proud of this Japanese colony in the United States.

explicit_text: Mr. Kyutaro Abiko was a founder of the Yamato Colony and a leader in the Japanese community. He also embarked on the newspaper business with Nichibei and invested all his earnings into the colony. He was a man of conviction and one of the founders of the Japanese Christian Church, recruiting people and selling land in lots.

explicit_text: My name is Shichinosuke Asano, born November 29, 1894, in Morioka, Iwate Prefecture. I came to the United States in 1918 at age 23 after working for the Tokyo Maiyu newspaper. I worked hard farming in California with my brother and later moved to San Francisco. I have lived through many changes and still feel young at heart despite my age.

explicit_text: After leaving Morioka, I went to Tokyo to find a senior from my hometown and attended school while working as a doorkeeper. I worked for the Tokyo Maiyu newspaper there before coming to the United States as a special correspondent. Tokyo was where I first encountered the idea of coming to America and where I prepared for my journey.

explicit_text: When I came to the United States, I noticed buildings like Kaijoh Biru in Tokyo were common here. Kaijoh Biru was a new tall building in front of Tokyo Station when I left Japan, and I was impressed by how culturally developed America was compared to Tokyo at that time. It symbolized the modernization I witnessed before emigrating.
